reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (24% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [Low risk] — 1 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 95/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  Guardian (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / UNICC Responsible AI): 17/100 [Critical risk] — 2 critical, 5 high, 3 medium

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — score below 30: Guardian (17/100)
  • REJECT trigger — critical findings in Guardian: [guardian-1] No governance documentation exists for VeriMedia AI's ResNet/BERT-based media verification system; [guardian-2] Autonomous authenticity and moderation decisions lack documented human oversight controls

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 95/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) — Δ45 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) while Guardian scored 17/100 (critical risk) — Δ33 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 95/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 17/100 (critical risk) — Δ78 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • Base score: 54/100 average across 3 module(s)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
Across modules, the strongest agreement is evidentiary: Sentinel (sentinel-1-a4245fc0) and Watchdog (watchdog-1) both conclude that the absence of source code, dependency manifests, and deployment configuration blocks a substantiated security/AI-risk review. They also converge implicitly on the likely broad attack surface (public Flask APIs + user-uploaded media + cloud deployment) but cannot validate baseline controls (authn/z, input validation, upload handling, rate limiting, secrets, dependency exposure) without artifacts.

The main conflicts are about how to translate missing evidence into an overall risk posture. Sentinel’s “low” risk score is effectively a data-quality limitation note, while Guardian treats missing governance artifacts and the described autonomous moderation/authenticity impact as inherently high/critical risk (guardian-1, guardian-2, guardian-5, guardian-6), independent of code visibility. Similarly, Watchdog limits itself to “info” due to unconfirmed LLM integration, whereas Guardian’s concerns remain severe even for non-LLM ML systems. Key gaps not adequately covered by any module include concrete AWS/cloud security hardening (IAM/S3/KMS/WAF/secrets), adversarial ML robustness threats specific to deepfake detection and claim verification, and risks around third-party/social ingestion and retrieval (provenance, ToS/consent, SSRF/unsafe fetch, and indirect content-based attacks).

top_findings: Insufficient source code provided for VeriMedia AI security analysis
sentinel.summary: VeriMedia AI is described as a Flask/Python media verification platform using REST APIs, PostgreSQL, AWS, and user-uploaded content processing, but no source code or dependency data was supplied. As a result, the security posture cannot be assessed beyond noting that the available information is insufficient for code-based analysis.
sentinel.recommendation: Top priority: provide the actual Flask/Python source tree, dependency manifests, and AWS/deployment configuration so concrete findings can be tied to specific files and lines.

top_findings: No source code provided — unable to verify any LLM integration or evaluate OWASP LLM Top 10 risks
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI is described as a Flask/Python AI-powered media verification platform using ResNet/BERT and autonomous moderation; however, no source code was available, and no explicit LLM integration can be confirmed (no OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini/LangChain/etc. evidence to analyze). Therefore, OWASP LLM Top 10 assessment cannot be performed to the required standard (file/line/snippet-backed findings).
watchdog.recommendation: 1) Provide the code artifacts needed to confirm/deny LLM usage (routes/services, background workers, dependency lockfiles) so OWASP-LLM01/02/07/08 can be evaluated with file/line evidence. 2) If any LLM is used for claim extraction/fact-checking/moderation, implement prompt injection defenses (strict message separation, content provenance labeling, and refusal policies) and add output validation before downstream actions. 3) If the LLM can call tools/APIs (social scraping, DB writes, moderation

top_findings: No governance documentation exists for VeriMedia AI's ResNet/BERT-based media verification system
Autonomous authenticity and moderation decisions lack documented human oversight controls
VeriMedia AI likely falls into a high-risk regulatory posture without any documented EU AI Act classification or FRIA
guardian.summary: VeriMedia AI presents a weak responsible-AI governance posture based on the materials provided: no repository files were available to evidence documentation, oversight, privacy controls, fairness testing, or accountability mechanisms. Given the stated use of ResNet and BERT for autonomous media authenticity and moderation decisions affecting public discourse, the absence of governance artifacts is itself a major compliance finding. The application appears exposed to significant NIST AI RMF, EU A
guardian.recommendation: 1. Immediately establish baseline governance documentation: create README.md, model cards for the ResNet and BERT components, privacy-policy.md, and ai-governance-policy.md to address the critical documentation gap and NIST-GOV-1 requirements. 2. Implement and document human oversight for all authenticity and moderation decisions, including manual review thresholds, override authority, appeal handling, and incident runbooks, to address the critical EUAI-OVERSIGHT finding. 3. Launch a formal risk
